coredata.dc:title: Laser induced anti-solvent carbon quantum dots in defect passivation for effective perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuhan
He, Zhiyuan
Li, Yang
Liu, Ke
Chen, Mengwei
Yang, Yingping
Li, Xiangyou
coredata.dc:description: In this study, anti-solvent carbon quantum dots (ASCQDs), generated via pulsed laser irradiation in chlorobenzene (CB), were adopted as an additive for defect passivation of the grain boundaries of CH3NH3PbI3 in hole-conductor-free, carbon-counter-electrode perovskite solar cells (PSCs). The size of the ASCQDs can be easily regulated by different laser fluence. The improvement of perovskite films’ morphology was revealed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM), which was beneficial for carrier transport. Higher photoluminescence (PL) intensity as well as prolonged carrier lifetime were proved in the ASCQDs optimized perovskite film, confirmed the laser induced ASCQDs can decrease nonradiative recombination via defect passivation, which can be further verified by the decreased defect density. As a result, the introduction of ASCQDs (1064 nm-50 mJ pulse−1 cm−1-10 min) led to a champion efficiency of 14.95%, which improved 14.04% when compared with pure CB treated PSCs (13.11%). Furthermore, the ASCQDs treated PSCs can still retain 93% and 81% of the initial power conversion efficiency after 600 h in the glovebox and in the atmosphere with humidity of 50–70%, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pulsed laser irradiation
Anti-solvent carbon quantum dots
Nonradiative recombination
Defect passivation
Perovskite solar cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, 11874167 ). The authors thank the Analytical and Testing Center of Huazhong University of Science and Technology for sample characterization and data measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the fabrication process of ASCQDs. The inset images illustrate the color change from anti-solvent CB to anti-solvent CQDs via laser irradiation. (b) TEM image of ASCQDs and HRTEM image as well as size distribution (inset). SEM images of (c) pristine and (d) with ASCQDs perovskite films. (e) cross-sectional SEM image of the ASCQDs optimized perovskite solar cell.
The whole XPS spectrum (a), Cl 2p peak (b)and C 1s peak (c) of the ASCQDs. (d) Binding energy of Pb 4f for pristine and ASCQDs optimized perovskite films.
PL spectra (a) and TR-PL spectra (b) of pristine and ASCQDs optimized perovskite films.

(a) Dark I-V curves of devices with the structure of FTO/perovskite/C. (b) Nyquist plots, (c) Light intensity versus voltage and (d) Light intensity versus voltage current density of Pristine and ASCQDs optimized PSCs.
Photovoltaic parameters of PSCs plotted as a function of different ASCQDs irradiated by various laser fluence (25, 50, 100, 150 and 200 mJ pulse−1 cm−2) for 10 min.
(a) Hysteresis J-V curves and (b) IPCE spectra of pristine and ASCQDs optimized perovskite solar cells.
Normalized PCE of unencapsulated PSCs based on the pristine and ASCQDs optimized perovskite films stored in (a) glove box and (b) air atmosphere (humidity: 50~70%).
Photovoltaic parameters of the devices based on the pristine and ASCQDs optimized perovskite films with different scan direction.

head.ce:title: Laser induced anti-solvent carbon quantum dots in defect passivation for effective perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, 1037 Luoyu Road, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, 1037 Luoyu Road, Wuhan 430074, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, School of Science, Wuhan University of Technology, 122 Luoshi Road, Wuhan 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, School of Science, Wuhan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, School of Science, Wuhan University of Technology, 122 Luoshi Road, Wuhan 430070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, anti-solvent carbon quantum dots (ASCQDs), generated via pulsed laser irradiation in chlorobenzene (CB), were adopted as an additive for defect passivation of the grain boundaries of CH3NH3PbI3 in hole-conductor-free, carbon-counter-electrode perovskite solar cells (PSCs). The size of the ASCQDs can be easily regulated by different laser fluence. The improvement of perovskite films’ morphology was revealed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM), which was beneficial for carrier transport. Higher photoluminescence (PL) intensity as well as prolonged carrier lifetime were proved in the ASCQDs optimized perovskite film, confirmed the laser induced ASCQDs can decrease nonradiative recombination via defect passivation, which can be further verified by the decreased defect density. As a result, the introduction of ASCQDs (1064 nm-50 mJ pulse−1 cm−1-10 min) led to a champion efficiency of 14.95%, which improved 14.04% when compared with pure CB treated PSCs (13.11%). Furthermore, the ASCQDs treated PSCs can still retain 93% and 81% of the initial power conversion efficiency after 600 h in the glovebox and in the atmosphere with humidity of 50–70%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have wide applications in photodetectors, light emitting diodes (LEDs) and photovoltaics et al. [1–6], due to their remarkable advantages such as high absorption coefficient [7], long carrier diffusion length [8] and tunable bandgap [9]. Although massive efforts have been made and observably improvements have been realized, many challenges still remain to be solved [10–15]. The migration of iodine and methylamine ion/defect will result in defects formation at the organic and inorganic halide perovskite materials’ surface. The nonradiative recombination can be hindered and the photoelectric properties of PSCs can be promoted by defect passivation. In fact, the carrier recombination, which occurs at the grain boundaries (GBs) of perovskite, is a fatal issue for the improvement of PSCs’ performance [16]. Generally, the carrier recombination, usually caused by the defects in GBs, can greatly influence the performance of PSCs [17]. In order to achieve better preparation of preeminent perovskite films, various anti-solvents [18–22], have been elaboratively investigated since the first report on exploiting anti-solvent for the performance enhancement of PSCs [19]. The first-class perovskite films have less defects, which can be passivated by different kinds of additives such as polymers, solvents and nanoparticles et al. [18,23–28]. Among these strategies, anti-solvents including passivation molecule additives have been particularly researched for obtaining perovskite films with outstanding photoelectric performance [25]. It is of great significance that such efforts as for the defects’ passivation effect as well as sluggish perovskite film degradation induced by reduced nonradiative recombination can be made. Besides, the performance of PSCs can be promoted by nanoparticle additives such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) that will simultaneously passivate GBs defects and benefit accelerating carrier transport [29–31]. With a surge of interest there are numerous researches about the applications of CQDs such as LEDs and sensitizers by virtue of its spectacular optical properties [32,33]. Furthermore, the hole transporting material, defects passivation of the perovskite films and dopant for the application in PSCs can be realized by these CQDs [34]. Based on the widely utilized anti-solvents method at present, a new strategy to combine the advantages of anti-solvents with inorganic nanomaterials such as CQDs to passivate defects in GBs and hinder carrier recombination of perovskite films seems scientific and reasonable, which contributes to the performance improvement of PSCs.

Unfortunately, it is incredibly tough for fabricating unambiguous defined nanoparticles in specific anti-solvents, and even the chemical methods will result in frequent occurrence residual grafted ligands on nanoparticles surface, which are not beneficial for applications such as optoelectronic [35,36], laser synthesis and processing of colloids (LSPC) can be a versatile and efficient way for nanoparticle fabrication, which has gained considerable development in the past decades [37–39]. Recently, laser generated Cl-CDs in the desired anti-solvents have been studied in Au-based planar PSCs with the highest power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 21.41% as well as scarcely efficiency loss after 5000 h preserved in a relative 40% humidity at indoor temperature [40]. However, driven by the advantages of low-budget, simplified and scalable fabrication, the influence of laser induced anti-solvent carbon quantum dots (ASCQDs) in the hole-conductor-free, carbon-electrode-based mesoporous PSCs needs to be elucidated.

In this work, we utilized pulsed laser irradiation to successfully fabricate ASCQDs in chlorobenzene (CB), which was favorable for the formation of superior perovskite films with uniform morphologies. Unlike the traditional chemical methods used in the majority works for CQDs fabrication, the pulsed laser irradiation method has many merits. For example, different sizes of uniform and stable CQDs can be fabricated via various laser fluence in a few minutes which is very fast and convenient. Besides, CQDs can be generated in the desired single liquid medium without extra additives that will introduce the unexpected ligands. In addition, CQDs will be formed in the anti-solvent and directly adopted to form a uniform optimized perovskite film. Thus, the pulsed laser irradiation method is appropriate for the fabrication of CQDs. Moreover, the uncoordinated lead ions can be effectively passivated by the functional groups such as hydroxyl and carbonyl at the surface of ASCQDs, thus to decrease nonradiative recombination, which will result in fewer defects and accelerated carrier transport in perovskite film. Consequently, the ASCQDs optimized hole-conductor-free, carbon-electrode-based mesoporous PSCs were fabricated with the maximum PCE of 14.95% and PSCs without encapsulated could retain 93% and 81% of their original PCE after being stored for 600 h in a glove box and in the atmosphere with 50–70% humidity at room temperature, much prior than those of the pristine PSCs (81% and 54%, respectively). This study was believed to bring great opportunities in various optoelectronic applications based on laser matter interaction.

Fig. 1a depicts the detailed pulsed laser irradiation process of the ASCQDs. In general, 4 mL anti-solvent of CB was injected into a glass bottle for irradiation by an unfocused laser beam (1064 nm, 200 mJ pulse−1 cm−2). During the irradiation process, the solution color changed from transparent to light yellow. After being laser irradiated, the colloidal solution containing CQDs exhibited an excitation wavelength dependent PL (Fig. S1a). As the excitation wavelength increases, the CQDs red shifted emission peak can be observed. The typical excitation dependent PL phenomenon, belonging to CQDs, can be attributed to the slowly solvent relaxation and multiple discrete electronic states [41]. Moreover, it is worth noting that the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S1b) of the suspension represented the same properties as CB, which proved the identical solution after laser irradiation. For the purpose of testifying the PL emitting phenomenon was from the CQDs instead of CB, ethanol was refilled in the glass bottle to form another suspension after the colloidal suspension was dried naturally (Fig. S2). The reappearance of the visible PL confirmed that the PL emitting phenomenon derived from CQDs rather than CB. As shown in Fig. 1b, the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image indicates the generation of nanoparticles with an average size of 3.43 nm. Besides, the lattice d-spacing of a single crystal is 0.213 nm, in accordance with the (100) lattice fringe of graphite [42]. Furthermore, the Raman spectra of the laser generated nanoparticles were characterized (Fig. S3a) and the peaks located at 1366 and 1588 cm−1 are ascribed to the D and G bands of CQDs. The results demonstrate that the CQDs can be easily fabricated in CB by laser irradiation. FT-IR (Fig. S3b) was performed to disclose the surface functional groups of CQDs. The small peak around 3440 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of COH. CH, CO and CC can be perceived at about 2920 cm−1, 1690 cm−1 and 1660 cm−1. In addition, CH2 stretching vibration peak was located at about 1483 cm−1. Thus, the surface hydrophobic functional groups of the CQDs can make perovskite film hydrophobic, which is favorable for long-term stability of the PSCs [43,44]. When the chemical process of the laser irradiation of CB was taken into consideration, the formation of CQDs can be ascribed to the fragmentation of CB, which is actuated by the electron produced in plasma induced by laser and the nanoparticles carbon framework reconstruction subsequently [40]. After two intermediate transition processes (polymerized, carbonized), the original products are finally transformed to graphitic carbon structures, which is akin to the laser irradiation of hexafluorobenzene [45]. As is known to all, nanoparticle additives are beneficial for defects passivation and crystallinity enhancement of the perovskite [30]. The SEM images (Fig. S4) present the morphological differences at the surface of the pristine and ASCQDs modified films. The ASCQDs film expresses full coverage and smoother surface (Fig. 1c and d), which is in correspondence with the decreased surface roughness verified by AFM (Fig. S5). In addition, as depicted in Fig. S4, the obvious decreasing of the surface roughness (Rq) value can be observed from 25.5 nm to 17.9 nm with the incorporation of ASCQDs, indicating the improved surface morphology. Furthermore, the structure of the PSCs (Glass/FTO/c-TiO2/m-TiO2/ZrO2/Perovskite/C) is revealed by the cross-sectional SEM image in Fig. 1e and the thickness of c-TiO2 and m-TiO2 layer, ZrO2/perovskite layer are 300 nm and 900 nm, separately. Moreover, ZrO2 is adopted as a scaffold to host the perovskite absorber and increases the stability of PSCs. Nevertheless, C is about 10 µm and not included in the cross-sectional SEM image in order to get clear insight into the thickness of another thinner layer.

We further utilized XPS to ascertain the composition of the as-prepared ASCQDs ( Fig. 2a). The peaks of Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 can be noticed in Fig. 2b for the laser generated ASCQDs. Moreover, the deconvolution of C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) was fitted to three peaks located at 284.8, 285.8 and 286.5 eV, which were assigned to CC, CC and CCl, respectively [46]. As shown in Fig. 2d, the two main binding energy peaks at 138.1 eV and 142.9 eV were assigned to Pb 4f7/2 and Pb 4f5/2 for the pristine perovskite film. However, the peaks of Pb shifted to higher binding energy for the ASCQDs modified film when compared with the reference film, which means that ASCQDs may anchor to the perovskite crystal surface by the interactions between the hydroxyl and carbonyl surface functional groups of ASCQDs and Pb2+ [47]. Furthermore, XRD spectra (Fig. S6) showed almost the same diffraction angles for pristine and ASCQDs modified films, reflecting that the lattice orientation and crystal structure of CH3NH3PbI3 was impervious by ASCQDs additive, which means the ASCQDs could distribute on the perovskite surface and GBs that has been proved by the reported work [47].

To understand the difference of carrier extraction and transport performance between MAPbI3 and ASCQDs modified film, steady-state PL and TR-PL were performed by depositing perovskite on glass substrate. As displayed in Fig. 3a, the fixed PL peaks were at about 772 nm. However, the ASCQDs modified film represented a higher PL intensity. It is known that the reduced PL intensity can be resulted from the nonradiative recombination, which is a major energy loss pattern [48]. Besides, the interactions of surface functional groups (hydroxyl and carbonyl) on ASCQDs with Pb2+ ions will effectively reduce the nonradiative recombination between the GBs [47]. Thus, the remarkable increase of the PL intensity demonstrated by ASCQDs additive film indicates less nonradiative recombination loss, which can be ascribed to passivation of GBs between perovskite crystals as the GBs are the main nonradiative recombination center. The above analysis can be further verified by the TR-PL results. As shown in Fig. 3b, the function for bi-exponential decay was adopted to match the PL lifetime (τ
i ) as well as the amplitudes (Ai) of the glass/perovskite devices due to the two different PL quenching patterns. Generally, the fast decay carrier lifetime τ1 is ascribed to the carriers’ bimolecular recombination, meanwhile the long decay carrier lifetime τ2 is assigned to trap-assisted recombination [49]. The parameters of TR-PL are shown in Table S1. Moreover, the PL decay lifetimes of pristine perovskite film sample are τ1 = 10.48 ns and τ2 = 45.13 ns along with 34% and 66% amplitudes, respectively. Whereas the ASCQDs modified film possesses higher PL decay lifetimes (τ1 = 28.55 ns and τ2 = 76.47 ns), which reflects the ASCQDs passivation could mainly decrease the traps at the surface and between the GBs of perovskite. The average carrier lifetime (τ
avg ) was used to evaluate the excited-state decay and free carrier recombination dynamics in the perovskite film, which can be calculated by the following equation [50].
(1)
τ
avg
=
∑
A
i
τ
i
2
∑
A
i
τ
i

The calculated results showed the average carrier lifetime of the pristine film and the ASCQDs optimized film are 41.43 ns and 61.60 ns, respectively. The longer carrier lifetime implies that nonradiative recombination was restrained by ASCQDs passivation, resulting in the less quenching possibility. Hence, the favorable performance can be achieved for the ASCQDs optimized PSCs.

In order to research the defect density (Nt) of the pristine as well as the ASCQDs optimized film, the space-charge-limited current (SCLC, in the dark mode) method was exploited with the structure of FTO/perovskite/C ( Fig. 4a). the defect density was calculated through the following equation [51].
(2)
N
t
= 2
ε
0
ε
r
V
TFL
/ q
L
2
Where e is the electron charge, L is the perovskite film thickness, ε0 is the vacuum permittivity and the relative dielectric constant (εr) of CH3NH3PbI3 is 32 [52]. The trap-filled limit voltages (VTFL) are acquired from Fig. 4a, which are about 0.96 V and 0.79 V for pristine and ASCQDs optimized films. Finally, the defect densities of the pristine and the ASCQDs optimized films are calculated to 3.73 × 1015 cm−3 and 3.07 × 1015 cm−3, respectively. In fact, the lower defect density of the ASCQDs optimized film indicates that the ASCQDs additive can passivate the defects efficiently. Moreover, the decreased defect density is in good agreement with the improved PL intensity and the prolonged carrier lifetime. The carrier transport and recombination process in PSCs were revealed by EIS spectra. As shown in Fig. 4b, the Nyquist plot includes two semicircles with high frequency and low frequency corresponding to the transfer resistance (Rtr) and recombination resistance (Rrec) [53,54]. Whereas, the resistance parameters are summarized in Table S2 after fitted with the equivalent circuit model displayed in the inset of Fig. 4b. The pristine film showed a higher Rtr (43 Ω) than the ASCQDs optimized one (8 Ω), illustrating a faster charge transport in ASCQDs modified film. Furthermore, a higher Rrec (261 Ω) was obtained with the ASCQDs optimized film, implying the suppressed carrier recombination. These results suggest a contribution for the faster carrier transport and less recombination with ASCQDs optimized film, implying the promoted carrier mobility in perovskite, which will be beneficial for the performance enhancement of PSCs.

To take a closer look at the carrier recombination property, the light intensity versus voltage and current density curves are conducted in an intensive research. As shown in Fig. 4c, in the open circuit mode, the photogenerated electron-holes will recombine entirely and the dynamic process can be demonstrated by the following equation [55].
(3)
V oc =
nKT
/
q ln
( P )
+ constant
Where n indicates ideality factor. K and T represent Boltzmann constant and Kelvin temperature, respectively. q is electronic charge. P is the light intensity. The recombination mechanism can be revealed by surveying the recombination close to Voc. Moreover, the bimolecular recombination will dominate if n is close to 1. On the other hand, the value of n will be greater than 1 if the defects occur. Thus, it is apparent that after ASCQDs optimization, the value of n is 1.027, which is closer to 1 than the pristine sample of 1.133, indicating the suppressed carrier recombination in defect states due to the interactions of surface functional groups on ASCQDs with Pb2+ ions. Besides, in the short circuit mode, the electron-holes will be separated to the electrodes by the internal voltage before recombination and the mechanism can be studied by the equation [56].
(4)
J
sc
∝
P
α
Where α is close to 1, indicating the bimolecular recombination degree. As depicted in Fig. 4d, for the ASCQDs optimized PSCs, the value of α is 0.993, closer to 1 than the pristine PSCs of 0.971, proving the decreased bimolecular recombination. Therefore, from the above results, the reduced defects are attributed to the passivated GBs, leading to the decreased carrier recombination and accelerated carrier transport, which will enhance the performance of PSCs.

To further elucidate the influence of intercalating ASCQDs into perovskite film on PSCs performance, pristine and ASCQDs optimized PSCs were fabricated and systematically investigated by photovoltaic tests. Moreover, the experiments demonstrate the adjustable size of the ASCQDs via changing the laser fluence (Fig. S7). With the laser fluence increasing from 0 to 200 mJ pulse−1 cm−2, the size of ASCQDs increases from 2.95 nm to 4.65 nm and subsequently decreases to 1.78 nm, revealing the laser fluence dependent phenomenon that has appeared in many works [57–59]. Thus, ASCQDs with different sizes were adopted to prepare PSCs, the photovoltaic parameters of which are plotted in Fig. 5 and listed in Table S3. The obtained PCE reaches the highest at 50 mJ pulse−1 cm−2-10 min with 1064 nm laser wavelength and the average size of ASCQDs is 3.43 nm. Besides, the average V oc, J sc, FF and PCE increase from 1.008 V to 1.032 V, 22.31 mA/cm2 to 23.66 mA/cm2, 54.37–58.58% and 12.23–14.29%, respectively. It should be noted that the improvements appear in all the parameters especially for the J sc and V oc. This was ascribed to the sufficient perovskite passivation, which results in the decreased nonradiative recombination and the accelerated carrier transport, as can be certified by the above characterization tests. However, with the laser fluence increasing, the performance of PSCs decreases obviously, which can be attributed to the excess amount of ASCQDs induced by higher laser fluence that could form clusters and lead to more carrier recombination as well as hindered carrier transport [60]. Finally, the ASCQDs optimized device showed a champion PCE of 14.95%, which improved 14.04% when compared to the pristine PSC (13.11%). The photovoltaic parameters (V oc, J sc, FF) of the champion PSC sample are 1.03 V, 23.84 mA/cm2 and 61.13%, separately. Furthermore, the fixed laser fluence with different laser irradiation times have also been probed and the champion PCEs are summarized in Table S4. At a desired laser fluence, the champion PCE for different laser irradiation time increases to the highest for 10 min and then decreases with the laser irradiation time increasing. Moreover, at a desired laser irradiation time, the champion PCE for different laser fluence increases to the highest at 50 mJ pulse−1 cm−2 and then decreases with the laser fluence increasing. Thus, the proper content and size induced by different laser fluence and irradiation time will be achieved at 50 mJ pulse−1 cm−2 for 10 min. The hysteresis J-V curves of pristine and ASCQDs optimized PSCs with different scan directions are displayed in Fig. 6a and the relevant J-V parameters are listed in Table 1. The hysteresis index was calculated by the following equation [61,62]:
(5)
h =
PCE
reverse
−
PCE
forward
/
PCE
reverse

After ASCQDs optimization, the hysteresis index decreased to 7.99%, which is half of the pristine PSC, indicating the suppressed hysteresis due to the passivation of the GBs and the carrier recombination decreased at the TiO2/perovskite interface [63]. Moreover, the corresponding IPCE spectra (Fig. 6b) exhibit an entire region enhancement and the integrated current density are 21.24 and 22.41 mA/cm2 for the pristine and ASCQDs optimized PSCs, respectively, which align with the J sc value. In addition, as shown in Fig. S8, the PSCs optimized with ASCQDs exhibits a continuous output current density of 21.41 mA/cm2 at the maximum power point in 200 s, higher than the pristine PSCs of 19.51 mA/cm2. The improvement of the current density was ascribed to the increased carrier extraction and transport, which could lead to a remarkable improvement of J sc and eventually the promoted performance of the PSCs.

The stability of the ASCQDs optimized PSCs is an essential factor for the commercialization of the PSCs. As shown in Fig. 7a, after 600 h preservation in the glove box, the PCE of the PSCs based on the pristine film retained 81% of the initial value, meanwhile the PCE of the PSCs prepared with ASCQDs optimized film kept 93% of the original PCE. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 7b, for the samples preserved in the atmosphere with humidity of 50–70% for 600 h, the PCE of the pristine PSCs decreased rapidly and only retained 54% of the original value. Nevertheless, in the same atmosphere condition, the PCE of the PSCs fabricated with ASCQDs optimized film can maintain 81% of the original PCE, which is more stable than the PSCs without optimization. Furthermore, the maximum power point tracking (MPPT) under a Xe lamp with light intensity of 100 mW cm−2 in 75% humidity air at 25 ℃ for 10 h of pristine and ASCQDs optimized PSCs are shown in Fig. S9. After continuous irradiation for 10 h, the ASCQDs optimized PSCs can still keep its original PCE of 26%, higher than the pristine PSCs of 13%. The excellent stability of the PSCs based on ASCQDs optimized film can be ascribed to the GBs’ passivation effect by ASCQDs. Through ASCQDs’ passivation, the defect densities decreased markedly for the perovskite film, which was beneficial for the carrier transport and performance enhancement of PSCs [47]. Besides, the surface hydrophobic functional groups of ASCQDs such as CH2 , CC and CO of in the GBs of the perovskite film can significantly resist the moisture [17,47].

In conclusion, ASCQDs were adopted as additive for perovskite film to passivate defect and promote the performance especially PCE and the stability of the PSCs. The ASCQDs additive results in better surface morphology and outstanding accelerated carrier transport. Whereas the passivation of the GBs reduces the intrinsic defect intensity, the interaction between surface functional groups (hydroxyl and carbonyl) of ASCQDs and Pb2+ ions will effectively reduce the nonradiative recombination between the GBs, which is confirmed by the promoted PL intensities, prolonged carrier lifetimes as well as the dark I-V curves of PSCs. Moreover, the embedding of ASCQDs in PSCs improved PCE from 13.11% to 14.95% and achieved excellent stability in the air condition, which retained 81% of its original PCE after being stored for 600 h in the atmosphere under 50–70% humidity in the dark and 93% of its original PCE after being preserved for 600 h in the glove box, demonstrating the prominent characteristics of the ASCQDs obtained by anti-solvents. By virtue of the pervasive and particular characteristics of the pulsed laser irradiation in liquid for the production of diverse nanoparticles in desired solvents, not only CQDs but also other nanoparticles even quantum dots, can be fabricated. This work also paves a way for the laser generated ASCQDs and even other anti-solvent nanomaterials application in hole-conductor-free, carbon-counter-electrode-based high efficiency and stable PSCs.

Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrates (1.5 cm * 1.5 cm, 15 Ω sq−1) were purchased from Advanced Election Technology Corp. Titanium (diisopropoxide) bis (2,4-pentanedionate) (TIPD, 75%) isopropanol solution, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO,>99.5%) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF,>99.5%) were traded from Sigma Aldrich. CB (>95%) was bought from Aladdin. Mesoporous TiO2 (30 NRT-D) was bought from Dyesol. Mesoporous ZrO2 (30 NRT-D) was purchased from Shanghai MaterWin New Materials Corp. PbI2 and CH3NH3I were bought from Xi’an Polymer Light Technology Corp.

4 mL CB was poured into a quartz glass vessel for irradiation by an Nd:YAG non-focusing pulsed laser (Quantel brilliant B, repetition rate: 10 Hz, pulse width: 10 ns, beam diameter: 9 mm) with a wavelength of 1064 nm for 10 min. During irradiation, in order to keep the solution uniform, a magnetic stirrer was used and the laser fluence varied from 0 to 200 mJ pulse−1 cm−2.

The glass/FTO substrates were cleaned by deionized water containing detergent, acetone, isopropanol, and ethanol for 30 min in sequence by ultrasonic wave, followed by drying and treated by ozone plasma for 20 min to eliminate organic residual. A 50 nm compact TiO2 (c-TiO2) layer was fabricated by spin-coating compact precursor solution (TIPD: ethanol = 1: 19). Then, mesoporous TiO2 (m-TiO2) paste (TiO2: ethanol = 1: 5) was spin-coated (3500 rpm, 20 s) and annealed for 30 min at 500 ℃. After that, ZrO2 paste (ZrO2: ethanol = 1: 5) was spin-coated (5000 rpm, 20 s) and annealed for 30 min at 500 ℃. After cooling the device down, ozone plasma was used to enhance the film wettability before perovskite precursor deposition. Subsequently, CH3NH3PbI3 layer was fabricated by spin-coating (1000 rpm, 10 s and 4000 rpm, 20 s) 30 μL perovskite precursor solution (231 mg PbI2, 79 mg CH3NH3I, 300 mg DMF, 39 mg DMSO). During the second step, 200 μL CB or ASCQDs-containing CB was immediately poured on the substrate 12 s prior to the end of the process to form an even perovskite film. Thereafter, the resulted substrate was put onto a hot plate for 10 min at 100 ℃. Finally, the device fabrication of PSCs was finished after three carbon counter electrodes were prepared by screen-printing and then annealed for 15 min at 100 ℃.

Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, FEI, Nova NanoSEM 450) and High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, FEI, Talos F200X) were used to characterize the morphology and structure of the samples. The crystal structure of PSCs was carried out by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, PANalytical, PW3040/60) at 40 KV and 40 mA using the Cu-Kα line (λ = 0.154443 nm) as the excitation source. The infrared absorption spectra were obtained by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker, VERTEX 70). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, ESCALAB 250Xi) measurements were determined by a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source at normal emission. The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (Shimazu, RF-6000), and the excitation wavelengths were 470 nm for the perovskite films. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TR-PL) was tested by a FLS 980E fluorometer. Surface roughness of the perovskite films were recorded by Atomic Force Microscope (AFM, Shimadzu, SPM9700). Current density-voltage (J-V) curves of the PSCs were obtained by using a solar simulator (Oriel, model 91192–1000) and Keithley 2400 source meter under standard one sun AM 1.5 G (100 mW cm−2) illumination with an effective area of 0.08 cm2. Incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectrum were performed by using a 150 W xenon lamp (Oriel) fitted with a monochromator (Cornerstone 74004). Electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were conducted by using the electrochemical workstation (Zahner, Kronach, Germany) with the frequency range from 10 mHz to 10 MHz under AM 1.5 G sunlight illumination and with a bias voltage of 1.0 V. The PSCs were measured under ambient conditions [20%< relative humidity (RH)< 50%] every time. After the measurements, the PSCs were stored in a glovebox.

Shuhan Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zhiyuan He: Investigation. Yang Li: Investigation. Ke Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Mengwei Chen: Investigation. Yingping Yang: Supervision. Xiangyou Li: Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, 11874167). The authors thank the Analytical and Testing Center of Huazhong University of Science and Technology for sample characterization and data measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.161561.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
